From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1984 American horror film

For other uses, see [Chud (disambiguation)](/wiki/Chud_(disambiguation) "Chud (disambiguation)").

| C.H.U.D. | |
| --- | --- |
| Theatrical release poster | |
| Directed by | Douglas Cheek |
| Screenplay by | [Parnell Hall](/wiki/Parnell_Hall_(writer) "Parnell Hall (writer)") |
| Story by | Shepard Abbott |
| Produced by | Andrew Bonime |
| Starring | * [John Heard](/wiki/John_Heard_(actor) "John Heard (actor)") * [Daniel Stern](/wiki/Daniel_Stern_(actor) "Daniel Stern (actor)") * [Christopher Curry](/wiki/Christopher_Curry_(actor) "Christopher Curry (actor)") |
| Cinematography | [Peter Stein](/wiki/Peter_Stein_(cinematographer) "Peter Stein (cinematographer)") |
| Edited by | [Claire Simpson](/wiki/Claire_Simpson "Claire Simpson") |
| Music by | * [Martin Cooper](/wiki/Martin_Cooper_(artist) "Martin Cooper (artist)") * [David A. Hughes](/wiki/Dave_Hughes_(musician) "Dave Hughes (musician)") |
| Distributed by | [New World Pictures](/wiki/New_World_Pictures "New World Pictures") |
| Release date | * August 31, 1984 (1984-08-31) |

| Budget | $1.25 million[[1]](#cite_note-numbers-1) |
| Box office | $4.7 million[[1]](#cite_note-numbers-1) |

***C.H.U.D.*** is a 1984 American [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction_film "Science fiction film") [horror film](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") directed by Douglas Cheek, produced by Andrew Bonime, and starring [John Heard](/wiki/John_Heard_(actor) "John Heard (actor)"), [Daniel Stern](/wiki/Daniel_Stern_(actor) "Daniel Stern (actor)"), and [Christopher Curry](/wiki/Christopher_Curry_(actor) "Christopher Curry (actor)") in his film debut. The plot concerns a New York City police officer and a homeless shelter manager who team up to investigate a series of disappearances, and discover that the missing people have been killed by humanoid monsters that live in the sewers.

The title of the movie is an abbreviation for "cannibalistic humanoid underground dwellers".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

*C.H.U.D.* was released in North America on August 31, 1984, and grossed $4.7 million. It was followed in 1989 by a sequel titled *[C.H.U.D. II: Bud the C.H.U.D.](/wiki/C.H.U.D._II:_Bud_the_C.H.U.D. "C.H.U.D. II: Bud the C.H.U.D.")*.

## Plot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Plot")]

A woman, Flora Bosch, is walking her dog down an empty, darkened city street. As she passes by a [manhole](/wiki/Manhole "Manhole"), a creature attacks Mrs. Bosch and drags her and the dog into the sewers.

George Cooper, a once-prominent fashion photographer, has since forgone fame and fortune and is living with his girlfriend Lauren. His current project is photographing New York City's [homeless](/wiki/Homeless "Homeless") population, specifically those known as "undergrounders", or people who reside in the city's sewers and subway tunnels.

[NYPD](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") Captain Bosch has a personal interest in the recent flood of missing persons being reported to his precinct, as Flora—his wife—is also missing. Bosch interviews A.J. "The Reverend" Shepherd, who runs the local homeless shelter. A.J. believes that the recent events are part of a massive government cover-up and has the evidence to prove it. Bosch's superiors know more than they are letting on and seem to be taking their cues from Wilson, who works for the [Nuclear Regulatory Commission](/wiki/Nuclear_Regulatory_Commission "Nuclear Regulatory Commission") (NRC).

It turns out that monsters are lurking beneath the streets: beings that were once human, but have been mutated by radioactive, chemical [toxic waste](/wiki/Toxic_waste "Toxic waste") into hideous, flesh-eating creatures that prey on the homeless who live in the underground. Given the recent drop in the underground transient population, the creatures have resorted to coming to the surface through sewer manholes to feed. Through a series of events, both George and A.J. find themselves trapped in the sewers, a reporter gets involved and eaten, and Lauren has a problem with both a clogged shower drain and a mutant that comes up through the sewer access point that she opened in the basement of her apartment building.

A.J. and George meet up and discover that the NRC is directly involved in the slaughter that has been going on. Although the political bureaucracy has forbidden the NRC to transport the toxic wastes through New York because of the large-scale danger to the public, it has secretly been hiding the waste by-products, marked as "**C**ontamination **H**azard **U**rban **D**isposal", beneath Manhattan in the abandoned subway tunnels. The underground homeless population has been coming into contact with these by-products, turning them into the mutated creatures. To protect this secret, Wilson plans to seal the sewers, open up some gas lines and asphyxiate the C.H.U.D.s and any witnesses of their existence, despite the inherent danger to the city. In the meantime, Lauren is attacked by the mutants in her apartment and narrowly escapes them.

Later that evening at a diner, two police officers and the waitress are killed and carried off by the mutants, finally drawing the public's attention to the disappearances. George and A.J. recover a camera set left behind by an NRC crew slain by the mutants during a previous clean-up attempt and use it to report their findings to Bosch. Confronted by Bosch, Wilson runs off, and later shoots Bosch while the latter helps A.J. and George escape from a manhole. Wilson then tries to run George and A.J. over with a truck, but A.J. fatally shoots Wilson with Bosch's gun, and the truck explodes as it drives into the manhole.

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Release")]

### Theatrical release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Theatrical release")]

*C.H.U.D.* was given a [limited release](/wiki/Limited_release "Limited release") theatrically by [New World Pictures](/wiki/New_World_Pictures "New World Pictures") beginning in August 1984. The film opened at #13 at the box office, grossing $1,762,922 with an average gross of $5,686 in 310 theaters.[[3]](#cite_note-weekendboxoffice-3) Its second week saw it dropping to #14 at the box office, with a gross of only $834,465 and a worldwide gross of $2,846,756.[[4]](#cite_note-weekendboxoffice2-4)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Home media")]

*C.H.U.D.* was released for home video on [Betamax](/wiki/Betamax "Betamax") and [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") by [Media Home Entertainment](/wiki/Media_Home_Entertainment "Media Home Entertainment") in 1984.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The film was released on DVD by [Anchor Bay Entertainment](/wiki/Anchor_Bay_Entertainment "Anchor Bay Entertainment") on January 30, 2001. The company released the film again in 2008 as a part of its *Cult Fiction* series.[[6]](#cite_note-allmoviedvd-6) [Image Entertainment](/wiki/Image_Entertainment "Image Entertainment") would release the film in 2011,[[7]](#cite_note-7) and in 2012, with the later release being a part of a two-disc multi-feature alongside *[Children of the Corn](/wiki/Children_of_the_Corn_(1984_film) "Children of the Corn (1984 film)")*, *[Creepshow 2](/wiki/Creepshow_2 "Creepshow 2")*, and *[House](/wiki/House_(1985_film) "House (1985 film)")*.[[6]](#cite_note-allmoviedvd-6) A two-disc [Special Edition](/wiki/Special_Edition "Special Edition") was released on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") by [Arrow Video](/wiki/Arrow_Video "Arrow Video") on November 22, 2016.[[6]](#cite_note-allmoviedvd-6)[[8]](#cite_note-specialedition-8) That version is now out of print. A new one disc Blu-ray was released on November 9, 2021.

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Reception")]

[Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes"), a [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator"), reports a 43% approval rating based on 49 reviews. The consensus summarizes: "It has its fair share of goopy B-movie thrills and unexpectedly pointed social commentary, but *C.H.U.D.* ultimately emerges from its cinematic sewer as a narratively slack and inconsistent creature feature."[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), which uses a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean"), assigned the film a score of 57 out of 100, based on 4 critics, indicating "mixed or average" reviews.[[10]](#cite_note-Metacritic-10)

[Lawrence Van Gelder](/wiki/Lawrence_Van_Gelder "Lawrence Van Gelder") from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* stated in his review for the film, "*C.H.U.D.* makes no pretension toward serious thesis about government or the environment. It is meant to be light commercial entertainment, and in the category of horror films it stands as a praiseworthy effort".[[11]](#cite_note-11) Keith Phipps of *[The A.V. Club](/wiki/The_A.V._Club "The A.V. Club")* wrote, "Perfect for bleary-eyed late-night viewing and pretty much unwatchable at any other hour."[[12]](#cite_note-12) Patrick Naugle of [DVD Verdict](/wiki/DVD_Verdict "DVD Verdict") called it a fun film that focuses more on entertainment than deeper issues.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Joshua Rothkopf of *[Time Out New York](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")* included it in *Time Out*'s list of best New York-set films, calling it "more funny than scary".[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Bloody Disgusting](/wiki/Bloody_Disgusting "Bloody Disgusting") rated it 4.5/5 stars and called it "definitely one of b-movies best kept secrets".[[15]](#cite_note-15)

It won Best Fantasy Film at [Brussels International Festival of Fantasy Film](/wiki/Brussels_International_Festival_of_Fantasy_Film "Brussels International Festival of Fantasy Film") in 1985.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Legacy")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/C.H.U.D. "Special:EditPage/C.H.U.D.") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(November 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A sequel, *[C.H.U.D. II: Bud the C.H.U.D.](/wiki/C.H.U.D._II:_Bud_the_C.H.U.D. "C.H.U.D. II: Bud the C.H.U.D.")*, was released in 1989.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Although *C.H.U.D.* was negatively received during its initial release, it attracted a [cult following](/wiki/Cult_film "Cult film") over the years, inspired the name of [a film website](/wiki/CHUD.com "CHUD.com")[[17]](#cite_note-17) (which changed the acronym's meaning to Cinematic Happenings Under Development) and a political epithet,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and references to it have appeared in *[The Simpsons](/wiki/The_Simpsons "The Simpsons")*,[[19]](#cite_note-19) [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")'s *[The Flash](/wiki/The_Flash_(2014_TV_series) "The Flash (2014 TV series)")*, *[Aqua Teen Hunger Force](/wiki/Aqua_Teen_Hunger_Force "Aqua Teen Hunger Force")*, *[Clerks II](/wiki/Clerks_II "Clerks II")*, *[Castle](/wiki/Castle_(TV_series) "Castle (TV series)")*, *[Archer](/wiki/Archer_(2009_TV_series) "Archer (2009 TV series)")*, *[Futurama](/wiki/Futurama "Futurama")*, *[Pushing Daisies](/wiki/Pushing_Daisies "Pushing Daisies"),* *[Outer Banks](/wiki/Outer_Banks_(TV_series) "Outer Banks (TV series)")*, *[Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D.](/wiki/Agents_of_S.H.I.E.L.D. "Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D.")*, and *[Rick and Morty](/wiki/Rick_and_Morty "Rick and Morty")*.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Martin Cooper's score was later named number 33 in *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")'*s 35 Greatest Horror Soundtracks.[[21]](#cite_note-greathorrorsoundtracks-21) The film was also the subject of an April Fool's hoax announcement by [The Criterion Collection](/wiki/The_Criterion_Collection "The Criterion Collection").[[22]](#cite_note-22) In 2007, [Rob Zombie](/wiki/Rob_Zombie "Rob Zombie") was rumored to be considering a remake,[[23]](#cite_note-23) and, in 2008, a different remake was rumored to be in production.[[24]](#cite_note-24) C.H.U.D. appears in the 2003 video game *[Tony Hawk's Underground](/wiki/Tony_Hawk%27s_Underground "Tony Hawk's Underground")* under the name T.H.U.D.[[25]](#cite_note-25) A VHS copy of *C.H.U.D.* appears in the opening shot of Jordan Peele's 2019 horror film *[Us](/wiki/Us_(2019_film) "Us (2019 film)")*.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The found footage horror film [*Dwellers*](/wiki/Dwellers_(film) "Dwellers (film)") has been publicly referenced by its writer and director [Drew Fortier](/wiki/Drew_Fortier "Drew Fortier") as being heavily influenced by C.H.U.D.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The anthology *C.H.U.D. LIVES!: A Tribute Anthology*, edited by Joe Mynhardt, was released in 2018 and featured an introduction by [David Drake](/wiki/David_Drake "David Drake"), an interview with the late C.H.U.D. movie producer Andrew Bonime, an interview horror author [Eric S. Brown](/wiki/Eric_S._Brown "Eric S. Brown") did with C.H.U.D. screenwriter [Parnell Hall](/wiki/Parnell_Hall_(writer) "Parnell Hall (writer)") and nineteen stories set in the universe of the movie C.H.U.D. Contributors included Robert E. Waters, Nick Cato, Ryan C. Thomas, David Robbins, Christopher Fulbright, Angeline Hawkes, Greg Mitchell, Alex Laybourne, Michael H. Hanson, Ben Fisher, [Tim Waggoner](/wiki/Tim_Waggoner "Tim Waggoner"), Jason White, Mort Castle, David Bernstein, Martin Powell, Chad Lutzke, J.G. Faherty, Phillip C. Perron, Ross Baxter, [Jonathan Maberry](/wiki/Jonathan_Maberry "Jonathan Maberry"), and Eugene Johnson.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The upcoming film *[Bunker Heights](/wiki/Bunker_Heights "Bunker Heights")* has been regarded by [Dread Central](/wiki/Dread_Central "Dread Central") as being a spiritual successor to C.H.U.D.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In late 2019, the term "[chud](/wiki/Chud_(pejorative) "Chud (pejorative)")" in reference to the film started to see usage in internet culture to derogatorily refer to individuals involved in [far-right politics](/wiki/Far-right_politics "Far-right politics").[[31]](#cite_note-chudjak-31) Years later, a variant of the [wojak](/wiki/Wojak "Wojak") meme modelled after [Patrick Crusius](/wiki/Patrick_Crusius "Patrick Crusius") titled "Chudjak" emerged. Like the contemporary usage of the word chud, the Chudjak meme has been used to satirise and mock users of [/pol/](/wiki//pol/ "/pol/") for their far-right politics.[[31]](#cite_note-chudjak-31)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C.H.U.D.&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: See also")]

* *[The Time Machine](/wiki/The_Time_Machine "The Time Machine")*, an 1895 novel featuring subterranean cannibals ("Morlocks") who prey on surface-dwellers.
* [Shaver mystery](/wiki/Richard_Sharpe_Shaver "Richard Sharpe Shaver"), a 1940s-era purportedly-true series of stories about subterranean cannibals ("Deros") who prey on surface-dwellers.
